verdict.answer_first: A leads closer to bottom line: "Yes, a few real problems"; B opens softer with "A few things" and delays the practical verdict.
verdict.decisiveness: A is less scattered: two paths and "exact replacement"; B surveys 3 paths: "cheap sanity check", "spec-correct-ish", "correctness actually matters".
verdict.fabrication: A makes a false JS claim: "In JavaScript `$` matches... before a final `\n`". B also uses an unverified number: "covers ~99% of typos".
verdict.actionability: B is easier to act on because it gives one replacement immediately plus trim guidance: "/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/" and "decide whether to `s.trim()` first".
